blurb:
Josie has floppy ears, a nubby little tail, and enough energy to keep up with basically anyone who wants a real adventure buddy. She's six years old, about 35 pounds, and a Hound mix who went through the Mostly Mutts training program and came out knowing what she's doing.

She was adopted once before, and it didn't work out because the family's pace didn't match hers. That's not a knock on Josie. That's just honest information. Her foster puts her energy somewhere around a 7 or 8 out of 10, but says her cuddle level runs just as high. She runs hard and then crashes next to you on the couch. If you like being active and also like a dog who actually wants to be near you, that's a pretty good deal.

She needs to be the only dog in the house, and cats are a no. For kids, her foster thinks 10 and up is a good fit since she plays with some mouthy enthusiasm, nothing mean, just enthusiastic. If you want to bring older kids in for a meet, Mostly Mutts encourages it.

Josie is in a foster home right now and ready to find her people. Visit mostlymutts.org to submit an inquiry, or stop by the shelter in Kennesaw.
scoring_notes:
• High-energy adventure dog with equally high cuddle scores is a compelling dual-appeal pitch for active adopters
• Previous adoption that failed due to lifestyle mismatch — not behavior — reframes her as a great dog still waiting for the right fit
• Returned once and restricted to solo-dog, no-cat homes suggests she has cycled through the system longer than average

blurb:
There is no other dog on this list who is part Boxer, part Corgi, and all personality. Asher is three years old, about 40 pounds, and has earned a reputation at the shelter for being genuinely goofy. That is not a marketing word. The volunteers actually use it. He's got a ginger brindle coat, a compact little body, and a lot going on upstairs.

He went through a prison training program and came out knowing how to behave and how to fetch. He will chase a ball, bring it back, and stare at you until you throw it again. His foster parents also say he's great at entertaining himself indoors, which is a rare and underrated quality. He likes walks, he likes people, and he likes to have a backyard to burn off steam.

A couple of honest notes: Asher does best as the only dog in the house. He's guarded toys and beds from other dogs before, and he can get reactive on leash when he spots one. His trainer will walk you through the introduction process if you want to do a meet. No cats, and kids 15 and up are the right fit since younger children make him a little uneasy. If that sounds like your house, he's worth going to meet.

Asher is currently in a foster home through Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue in Kennesaw. Head to their website to start an inquiry or stop by during adoption hours.
scoring_notes:
• Prison training program backstory is a proven newsletter hook that drives reader curiosity and community goodwill
• Boxer-Corgi mix is a genuinely rare combination that stops scrollers and sparks conversation
• Restrictions on dogs, cats, and young children significantly narrow his fit, pointing to a longer wait for the right home

blurb:
Bolton came into Mostly Mutts underweight, with thin fur, and some rough miles behind him. That was then. He's nine years old now, 56 pounds, and his foster says he lights up every room he walks into. That's a big claim, but it checks out based on everything else in his profile.

He's a Hound and Boxer mix, which means he has a nose that runs the show and a personality that keeps everyone entertained. He sniffs everything, plays tag with other dogs by running at them and then bolting away hoping they'll chase him, and is just as happy to drop onto the couch and hang out once the exploring is done. His foster describes him as sweet, gentle, and a little goofy. He's warmed up to kids around 8 to 12 in his foster home and liked them. No toddlers, and no cats since he'll chase them, but a calm dog who likes to play? Bring them in for a meet.

For anyone who has thought about adopting a senior dog and wasn't sure, Bolton is the argument for it. He's already trained, he's not wired like a puppy, and he just wants to be somewhere warm with people who are kind to him. He's been through enough. He deserves a soft landing.

Bolton is in a foster home through Mostly Mutts in Kennesaw. Visit mostlymutts.org to start the process.
scoring_notes:
• Senior dog comeback story — arrived underweight and rough, now the life of every room — is the most emotionally powerful arc in this batch
• Nine-year-old Hound-Boxer with goofy charm directly challenges reader hesitation about senior adoption, making him a newsletter centerpiece
• Age and history of neglect make him statistically one of the hardest profiles to place, giving this feature real urgency

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
Gaia spent a stretch of time on her own outside before a kind stranger took her in so she could have her kittens somewhere safe. Mostly Mutts helped find homes for the whole litter. Now her mom days are behind her, she's spayed, and she's ready to have someone actually take care of her for a change.

She's about two years old and has strong opinions about the daily routine. Her foster says she's already figured out exactly when the Greenies come out at night and when the bedtime Churu snack is supposed to happen, and she will absolutely remind you if you're running behind. She also likes to chat. If you want a cat who will fill you in on everything that happened while you were at work, she's got a lot to say.

She's not a lap cat, and she's clear about that. Pets and attention are welcome on her schedule, not yours. She's also completely fine entertaining herself when you're busy, which makes her a great fit for someone who wants real companionship without a cat who needs constant interaction. Her foster thinks she'd do best in a quieter home, with older kids or adults who get that kind of personality.

Gaia is with Mostly Mutts in Kennesaw. Contact them by phone or email to meet her.
scoring_notes:
• Survivor-turned-homebody arc from stray mother to quirky companion is a compelling human-interest hook
• Strong personality detail — snack-time enforcer and chatty housemate — gives readers a vivid, memorable character
• Niche fit for adults-only or quiet homes narrows her pool, suggesting she may have been waiting a while

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
Iceman was adopted from Mostly Mutts as a kitten and spent five years with a family that loved him. Then their situation changed, and he ended up back at the rescue through no fault of his own. He's five years old now, 11 pounds, fully grown, and just waiting for someone to give him a second chapter.

He's not bouncing off the walls or demanding constant attention. He likes hanging out with his people and watching the world go by. His foster is still getting to know him, but his previous family had him for years and clearly he was easy to live with. He's litter trained, up to date on vaccines, neutered, and microchipped. Just a solid, settled cat who already knows how to be a good housemate.

One thing worth knowing: he wasn't a fan of other male cats in his previous home. A female cat companion could work well, and he's shown he can do great with kids too. He loved a houseful of them in a previous foster placement. Dogs are an unknown, but a calm meet and greet would sort that out quickly.

He's in Kennesaw with Mostly Mutts. His adoption fee is $175. Reach out by phone or email to meet him.
scoring_notes:
• Low-maintenance, fully vetted adult cat with a proven five-year track record is an easy sell for busy households
• "Second chapter" narrative after a loving home loss resonates emotionally with readers who value loyalty
• Returned after five years implies a recent intake, but settled adult cats often wait longer than kittens

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
Gina came into rescue as part of a litter of kittens, got adopted, and then life got complicated. She started having stomach issues, her family returned her, and she spent the next several months getting poked and prodded trying to figure out what was going on. The vets still don't have a clean answer, but she's doing better, she's happy, and her foster calls her the best cat ever.

She's about 11 months old and only 5 pounds, and her foster says she's likely to stay small. If you've ever wanted a cat that keeps a little of that kitten energy into adulthood, Gina checks that box. She's playful, she's sweet, and she does love a good snuggle when the mood hits, even though she's pretty independent the rest of the time.

The one real consideration is food. She's on a special diet to keep her stomach settled, which means if you already have cats at home, you'd need a plan to feed her separately. It's not complicated, just something to think through. She's done well with the cats in her foster home and met a leashed dog without blinking, so with the right setup she can share a home. She also likes kids, as long as they're gentle with her.

Gina is at Mostly Mutts in Kennesaw. Reach out by phone or email to set up a meet, and ask about her by name.
scoring_notes:
• Unique "forever kitten" appeal with small size and playful energy makes her a standout adoption story
• Returned after medical mystery adds emotional depth and reader empathy without a resolved diagnosis
• Months of vet visits and a return suggest extended time in rescue, adding urgency to her feature
